one_liner: A 52-year-old man presents with a six-month history of fragile, blistering skin on the backs of his hands that worsens after sun exposure, along with darker urine.

stage2.hpi_extension: He first noticed that the skin on the dorsum of his hands and forearms became fragile and would tear with minor trauma. Over weeks he developed multiple painless tense blisters in sun-exposed areas that healed with scarring and milia. He has also noticed increased hair growth over his temples and cheeks. His urine has been a darker tea color. He drinks 8–10 beers daily and was diagnosed with chronic hepatitis C ten years ago. He denies abdominal pain, neurologic symptoms, or psychiatric disturbance.
stage2.pmh: Chronic hepatitis C (untreated), hereditary hemochromatosis (paternal uncle), heavy alcohol use
stage2.family_history: Father had 'sensitive skin' and similar blistering. Paternal uncle with hereditary hemochromatosis.
stage2.social_history: Construction worker with significant outdoor sun exposure. Drinks 8–10 beers daily. Smokes half a pack per day.
stage2.physical_exam: Skin: Multiple tense vesicles and bullae over the dorsum of both hands and forearms in a sun-exposed distribution; several healed scars and white milia from prior lesions. Skin fragility — gentle rubbing of the dorsal hand causes superficial sloughing. Hyperpigmentation of sun-exposed skin. Hypertrichosis of the temples and malar cheeks. Sclerodermoid (woody) thickening of the dorsal hand skin. Hepatomegaly with a firm liver edge palpable 4 cm below the costal margin. No abdominal tenderness.

lab_results.result: Color: dark tea / pink-orange. Examination under a Wood's lamp shows coral-pink fluorescence. Markedly elevated urine uroporphyrins and 7-carboxyporphyrins. Normal urine PBG and ALA.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Elevated urine uroporphyrins with NORMAL PBG and ALA distinguishes porphyria cutanea tarda from the acute hepatic porphyrias. Coral-pink fluorescence under Wood's lamp is a classic bedside finding.

lab_results.result: AST 88, ALT 102 (elevated), alk phos mildly elevated, total bilirubin normal. Ferritin 1240 ng/mL (markedly elevated), transferrin saturation 78% (high). Glucose 132 (mildly elevated).
lab_results.clinical_significance: Iron overload (high ferritin, high transferrin saturation) is a hallmark of PCT and is a major triggering factor — most patients have associated hepatic siderosis. Concurrent hepatitis C and alcohol use further drive the iron overload and uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase inhibition.

lab_results.result: Skin biopsy of the dorsal hand: subepidermal blister with a 'festooned' base (the dermal papillae project upward into the cavity). PAS-positive material in vessel walls. Direct immunofluorescence: IgG and complement at the dermal-epidermal junction and around vessels.
lab_results.clinical_significance: The 'festooned' subepidermal bulla and PAS-positive perivascular deposits are classic histologic findings of porphyria cutanea tarda.

stage5.final_clue: He is started on therapeutic phlebotomy to reduce iron stores and counseled to stop alcohol, avoid sun exposure, and pursue treatment for his hepatitis C. His skin lesions slowly improve over several months.

debrief.classic_presentation: Porphyria cutanea tarda is the most common porphyria. It classically presents in middle-aged adults with photosensitive skin findings — painless tense vesicles and bullae on the dorsa of the hands and other sun-exposed areas, with skin fragility, scarring, milia, hyperpigmentation, and hypertrichosis. Tea-colored urine that fluoresces coral-pink under Wood's lamp is characteristic. Major triggers include alcohol, hepatitis C, HIV, estrogen, smoking, and iron overload (hemochromatosis or HFE mutations). Unlike the acute porphyrias, there are NO neurologic, psychiatric, or abdominal symptoms.
debrief.pathophysiology: PCT is caused by reduced activity of uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase (UROD), the fifth enzyme in the heme biosynthesis pathway. Most cases are sporadic (Type I) due to acquired hepatic UROD inhibition; Type II is autosomal dominant from a UROD gene mutation. Iron overload, alcohol, hepatitis C, HIV, and estrogens all inhibit hepatic UROD activity. The resulting accumulation of uroporphyrinogen and 7-carboxyporphyrinogen — porphyrin intermediates that are deposited in the skin — react with sunlight (UV in the 400 nm Soret band) to generate reactive oxygen species that damage the dermal-epidermal junction, producing the characteristic blisters.
debrief.treatment_overview: Cornerstones: avoid all triggers (alcohol, estrogens, smoking, sun exposure), strict sun protection (clothing, broad-spectrum sunscreen — chemical sunscreens that block UVA work better than UVB-only blockers). Therapeutic phlebotomy is first-line to reduce iron stores. Low-dose hydroxychloroquine (or chloroquine) is an alternative that complexes with hepatic porphyrins and promotes their urinary excretion. Treat underlying hepatitis C with direct-acting antivirals — this often induces remission. Screen for HFE mutations and HCV in all patients with PCT.
high_yield_pearls: PCT is the MOST COMMON porphyria. Adults with photosensitive blisters on the backs of the hands, hyperpigmentation, hypertrichosis, and tea-colored urine.
Enzyme defect: uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase (UROD), the fifth enzyme in heme biosynthesis. Mostly acquired (sporadic, Type I), occasionally familial (Type II, AD).
Triggers: alcohol, hepatitis C, HIV, estrogens, smoking, iron overload (HFE mutations, hemochromatosis).
Urine fluoresces coral-pink/red under Wood's lamp. Elevated urine uroporphyrins, NORMAL PBG and ALA (distinguishes from AIP).
Treatment: phlebotomy (reduces iron) is first-line; avoid triggers; low-dose hydroxychloroquine; treat underlying HCV with DAAs.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Hematology and Oncology chapter, 'Porphyrias' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Biochemistry chapter, 'Heme synthesis pathway' figure.
